design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate through industrial and infernal level designs, acquire weapons and ammo, eliminate waves of demons using learned combat tactics and spatial awareness
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (ammo/health) against escalating demon density and difficulty spikes; adaptation required between TNT and Plutonia's distinct design philosophies
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Elite space marine pushing back demonic invasion through firepower and tactical positioning; avenging fallen comrades
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Significantly harder than predecessor Doom II despite using identical mechanics—difficulty comes from level design philosophy rather than new systems. Two distinct episodes represent opposing design philosophies: TNT: Evilution emphasizes industrial UAC bases and measured pacing; The Plutonia Experiment prioritizes relentless demon placement and forced engagements

difficulty.continues_system: Checkpoint-based progression with ability to restart from level beginning
difficulty.checkpointing: Per-level checkpointing; no mid-level saves in original design
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Difficulty selection at start (though higher difficulties increase demon count/intelligence); player must memorize level layouts and manage ammo economy. Plutonia Experiment notably more punishing than TNT: Evilution, offering implicit difficulty scaling through episode selection

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon progression through pickup discovery (pistol → shotgun → SSG → chaingun → plasma rifle → BFG); armor/health pickups; temporary invulnerability/invisibility power-ups
progression.unlock_mechanism: Linear level-by-level progression through 32-level megawads; two separate 32-level campaigns (TNT and Plutonia) available from main menu
replayability_hooks: Two thematically distinct campaigns with different design sensibilities
Speedrunning potential through learned level routes
Multiplayer modes on same level set
Skill-based challenge of harder difficulties (Plutonia significantly more demanding than TNT)

world_building_techniques: Environmental storytelling through level design (UAC bases, Hell architecture, progression from industrial to demonic)
Minimal cutscenes; plot conveyed through level themes and visual context
TNT: Evilution—demonic spaceship invasion of Jupiter moon base; player witnesses facility overrun and seeks vengeance
The Plutonia Experiment—secret research facility developing Quantum Accelerator to prevent future invasions
Both episodes set after Doom II events; narrative of escalating human-demon conflict

boss_design.philosophy: Iconic encounter demons treated as ultra-dangerous variants rather than narrative boss fights; Icon of Sin serves as final level's climax in both episodes. Boss encounters emerge from standard enemy pool scaled up; no unique boss-only mechanics introduced
notable_bosses: Icon of Sin (both episodes)—massive skull-faced demon serving as climactic encounter
Cyberdemon—ultra-heavy tank demon, critical threat; notably removed from some PSX levels due to technical constraints
Spider Mastermind—removed from PSX version; appears in PC version
Arch-vile—powerful resurrection demon; removed from PSX version
Multiple demons handled as mini-bosses within standard level flows rather than isolated boss encounters

faction_indicators: UAC (humans attempting dimensional gateway tech; institutional overreach theme)
Hell/Demonic forces (invasion threat; corruption of human technology)
Reformed UAC under new management (The Plutonia Experiment—institutional learning and defensive posture)
Space marines (human resistance; military professionalism in face of alien threat)
danger_signals: Demonic spaceship invasion of human moon base (TNT: Evilution)
Dimensional gateway experiments spiraling into catastrophic failure
Hell's continued strength post-Earth invasion (Plutonia context)
Weaponization of demonic technology/Quantum Accelerator development
Progressive escalation from industrial bases into Hell itself
environmental_storytelling: UAC facility design (functional, military aesthetic) contrasted with demonic corruption
Sky textures indicating shift from human space to hellish dimensions
Level progression from bases through infernal architecture representing dimensional breach
Resource scarcity in environments reflecting siege conditions
Corpses and destruction indicating prior battles and demonic occupation

sources_used: wikipedia—Final Doom article covering gameplay mechanics, difficulty assessment, both episode plots, development credits, and PSX port differences
